figures.caption: Figure 4 [Testbed] 17B dense model iteration time, normalized to no oversubscription baseline.
Figure 5 [Testbed] Iteration time for PP-out and DPout across varying number of microbatches.

Figure 14 [Simulation] Impact of cross-building latency on topologies with different number of GPUs per building. Iteration time normalized against the baseline scenario of A1 and B1 under 50 µs link latency.

Figure 13 [Simulation] Impact of cross-building oversubscription on topologies with different number of GPUs per building. Iteration time normalized against the baseline scenario of A1 and B1 under their respective default crossbuilding oversubscription ratio.
Figure 19 [Simulation] Iteration times normalized against default case with a 50 µs link latency. Impact of having heterogeneous link latency due to varying distances between regions.

Figure 20 [Simulation] Comparison of ECMP and Packet Spraying results with various number of Queue Pairs (QP). Realistic packet spraying that accounts for in-flight data limitation struggles under long latencies.
Figure 1 Scale-across model training infrastructure (Si et al., 2026; Meta, 2025). RTSW: Rack Training Switch; CTSW: Cluster Training Switch; ATSW: Aggregation Training Switch.
Figure 6 [Simulation] Production trace based simulations.

Figure 12 [Simulation] Impact of cross-building latency on topologies with different intra-building oversubscription ratio. Iteration time normalized against the baseline scenario of A1 and B1 under 50 µs link latency. In the bottom figure, B2 (instead of B1) is placed on the left because B2 has a lower oversubscription ratio (1:1).

Figure 11 [Simulation] Impact of cross-building oversubscription on topologies with different intra-building oversubscription ratio. Iteration time normalized against the baseline scenario of A1 and B1 under their respective default cross-building oversubscription ratio. In the bottom figure, B2 (instead of B1) is placed on the left because B1 has a lower oversubscription ratio (1:1).
Figure 18 [Simulation] PP-out is more sensitive to packet loss. Higher link latencies substantially increase the cost of lost packet retransmissions, resulting in slower training.
Figure 17 Completion time and throughput of NCCL Send-Recv between a pair of GPUs AllReduce among 16 GPUs located in two clusters at varying distances.
Figure 8 [Testbed] 40B MoE model iteration time, normalized to baseline of 1:1.33 oversubscription ratio. PP-out yields faster iterations when the oversubscription ratio is larger than 1:4.

Figure 3 A comparison of different pipeline schedules using an example setup with 4 PP ranks, 2 model chunks per PP rank (8 chunks in total), and 8 microbatches: (a) Llama 3 Flexible PP Chu et al. (2025), (b) DoraPP, (c) Interleaved ZBV Qi et al. (2024). Within each pipeline schedule, each row represents a PP rank, each column represents a time unit and dashed lines show propagation of the first microbatch.

Figure 24 [Testbed] 17B-Dense model training iteration speedup over the production configuration under various oversubscription ratios. At a high oversubscription ratio, ScaleAcross Explorer achieves a 2.83× speedup over the production baseline while Sailor achieves 2.36×.

Figure 23 [Testbed] 40B-MoE model training iteration speedup over the production configuration under various oversubscription ratios. At a high oversubscription ratio, ScaleAcross Explorer achieves a 2.19× speedup over the production baseline while Sailor achieves 1.69×.
Figure 22 ScaleAcross Explorer workflow overview.
Figure 15 [Testbed] Performance comparison between FSDP and HSDP. Iteration time are normalized to FSDP-out (microbatch size of 4).
Figure 16 [Testbed] PP-out iteration time with interleaved ZBV and DoraPP schedules. DoraPP increases cross-building communication frequency and is less effective under high oversubscription ratios.
Figure 27 Visualization of the emulation settings for DP-out, PP-out, and the hierarchical communication pattern of HSDP.

Table 3 Overview of network topologies used in simulations. "1:1" stands for no bandwidth oversubscription. The circled number in the "Configuration Space" column corresponds to the circled number in Figure 1 and represents the scale-across network configuration we simulate with each topology.
Figure 9 [Simulation] Training time comparison between MoE models with varying number of experts and dense model, under parallelism configuration TP4-PP16-DP128.
Figure 10 [Simulation] Training time comparison between MoE models with varying number of experts and the dense model, under parallelism configuration TP4-PP2-DP1024.

Figure 25 [Simulation] Training iteration speedup over the production trace configuration. ScaleAcross Explorer achieves an up to 1.09× speedup over the production baseline under various oversubscription ratios and latencies. Sailor is not optimized for long-context training and performs poorly; we omit it here to avoid skewing the bars.

Figure 26 [Simulation] Training iteration speedup over the production trace configuration, including Sailor results. A speedup less than 1.0 signals that the training iteration becomes longer than that of the production baseline. Sailor is at least 2.32× slower than the production baseline under various oversubscription ratios and latencies.
